expanded_description: A comprehensive examination of mitochondria and chloroplasts as the primary energy-converting organelles in eukaryotic cells, detailing their evolutionary origins through endosymbiosis, structural architecture, biochemical mechanisms of ATP synthesis and carbon fixation, genetic autonomy, and coordinated roles in cellular metabolism, signaling, and organismal energy homeostasis.

core_concept.principle: Mitochondria and chloroplasts are semi-autonomous organelles derived from ancient endosymbiotic events that convert energy through chemiosmotic mechanisms—mitochondria oxidize organic molecules to produce ATP via oxidative phosphorylation, while chloroplasts capture light energy to fix carbon and generate ATP/NADPH via photosynthesis.
core_concept.explanation: Both organelles share remarkable similarities reflecting their common bacterial ancestry: double-membrane envelopes, their own circular DNA genomes, 70S ribosomes, and the ability to replicate independently of the cell cycle. Mitochondria likely originated from an α-proteobacterium engulfed by an ancestral archaeon ~2 billion years ago, enabling aerobic respiration and the evolution of complex multicellular life. Chloroplasts arose later (~1.5 billion years ago) from primary endosymbiosis of a cyanobacterium by a eukaryotic host, enabling photosynthetic autotrophy in plants and algae. Both organelles use chemiosmosis—the generation of proton gradients across membranes to drive ATP synthesis via ATP synthase—demonstrating a universal mechanism for biological energy conversion. Mitochondria generate ~30-32 ATP per glucose molecule through the citric acid cycle and electron transport chain, while chloroplasts convert light energy into chemical energy through light-dependent reactions and the Calvin cycle. These organelles are not isolated energy factories but integrated metabolic hubs that communicate with the nucleus via retrograde signaling, coordinate with peroxisomes and the cytosol for metabolite exchange, and regulate cellular processes including apoptosis, calcium signaling, and stress responses. Dysfunction in mitochondrial or chloroplast function underlies numerous diseases including mitochondrial myopathies, neurodegenerative disorders, and metabolic syndromes.

image.alt: Side-by-side transmission electron micrographs and labeled diagrams of a mitochondrion and chloroplast at identical magnification
image.caption: Mitochondrion (left) showing outer membrane, inner membrane with cristae folds, matrix containing enzymes and mtDNA nucleoids. Chloroplast (right) showing outer/inner envelope, thylakoid membranes stacked into grana, stroma containing chloroplast DNA and Calvin cycle enzymes. Both organelles display double-membrane architecture reflecting endosymbiotic origin.

sections.section_title: Introduction: Endosymbiotic Origins and Evolutionary Significance
sections.introduction: Mitochondria and chloroplasts represent two of the most transformative evolutionary innovations in the history of life. The acquisition of mitochondria through endosymbiosis enabled eukaryotic cells to harness the high energy yield of aerobic respiration (~30 ATP per glucose vs. ~2 ATP from glycolysis alone), providing the metabolic foundation for cellular complexity, multicellularity, and the evolution of energy-intensive tissues like muscle and nervous system. The later acquisition of chloroplasts through primary endosymbiosis enabled the evolution of photosynthetic eukaryotes (plants and algae), fundamentally altering Earth's atmosphere through oxygen production and forming the base of most food chains. Secondary and tertiary endosymbiotic events further diversified photosynthetic lineages, with some algae acquiring chloroplasts by engulfing other eukaryotic algae. These organelles retain molecular fossils of their bacterial ancestry: circular genomes, bacterial-type ribosomes, N-formylmethionine initiation of protein synthesis, and sensitivity to antibiotics that target bacteria. However, over billions of years of coevolution, most ancestral genes were transferred to the nuclear genome (endosymbiotic gene transfer), leaving modern organelles with reduced genomes that encode only a handful of essential proteins. This creates a unique dependency: organelles require nuclear-encoded proteins imported via sophisticated targeting machinery, while the nucleus depends on organelle function for energy and metabolites. Understanding these organelles is essential for comprehending cellular energetics, evolutionary biology, human disease, and biotechnological applications from bioenergy to medicine.

introduction_image.alt: Evolutionary timeline showing endosymbiotic events from last eukaryotic common ancestor to modern plants and animals
introduction_image.caption: Timeline: ~2 BYA—mitochondrial endosymbiosis (α-proteobacterium); ~1.5 BYA—primary chloroplast endosymbiosis (cyanobacterium); ~1 BYA—secondary endosymbiosis events creating diverse algal lineages. Gene transfer from organelles to nucleus continued over evolutionary time, creating modern dependency.

key_concept.definition: Mitochondria and chloroplasts are semi-autonomous, double-membrane-bound organelles of endosymbiotic origin that convert energy through chemiosmotic mechanisms—mitochondria via oxidative phosphorylation and chloroplasts via photosynthesis—while retaining reduced genomes, bacterial-type ribosomes, and independent replication capacity.
key_concept.context: These organelles exemplify the endosymbiotic theory proposed by Lynn Margulis (1967), supported by overwhelming molecular, structural, and genetic evidence. Their study bridges cell biology, evolutionary biology, biochemistry, and medicine, revealing how ancient symbiotic events shaped modern eukaryotic life.

image.alt: Diagram summarizing evidence for endosymbiotic origin of mitochondria and chloroplasts
image.caption: Evidence includes: double membranes (inner from symbiont, outer from host vesicle), circular DNA without histones, 70S ribosomes, bacterial-type protein synthesis (N-formylmethionine), antibiotic sensitivity, phylogenetic similarity to α-proteobacteria (mitochondria) and cyanobacteria (chloroplasts), and independent binary fission replication.

sections.key_concept: Mitochondria and chloroplasts function through four integrated systems: (1) membrane architecture for compartmentalization and gradient generation, (2) electron transport chains for energy conversion, (3) ATP synthase for chemiosmotic coupling, and (4) genome expression systems for semi-autonomous protein synthesis.

classifications.value: Generate ATP through oxidative phosphorylation by oxidizing organic molecules (pyruvate, fatty acids, amino acids) using oxygen as terminal electron acceptor; found in nearly all eukaryotic cells
characteristics: Double-membrane envelope: outer membrane (permeable porins), inner membrane (impermeable, folded into cristae)
Four compartments: outer membrane, intermembrane space, inner membrane, matrix
Matrix contains citric acid cycle enzymes, mtDNA nucleoids, mitochondrial ribosomes, and metabolic intermediates
Inner membrane contains electron transport chain complexes (I-IV), ATP synthase (Complex V), and metabolite transporters
Dynamic organelles undergoing continuous fission (Drp1) and fusion (Mfn1/2, OPA1) to maintain health and distribute throughout cell
classifications.behavior: Mitochondria generate proton motive force (Δp = ΔΨ + ΔpH) across inner membrane through electron transport from NADH/FADH₂ to oxygen. Protons flow back through ATP synthase, driving rotation of the γ-subunit and conformational changes that synthesize ATP from ADP + Pi. Mitochondria also regulate calcium homeostasis, generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) as signaling molecules, and initiate apoptosis via cytochrome c release. Metabolic flexibility allows switching between carbohydrate, fat, and protein oxidation based on nutrient availability.

examples.process: Oxidative Phosphorylation via Electron Transport Chain
examples.explanation: NADH from citric acid cycle donates electrons to Complex I (NADH dehydrogenase), which pumps 4 H⁺ into intermembrane space. Electrons pass to ubiquinone (Q), then Complex III (cytochrome bc₁, pumps 4 H⁺), then cytochrome c, then Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase, pumps 2 H⁺) where oxygen is reduced to water. FADH₂ from succinate dehydrogenase (Complex II) enters at Q, bypassing Complex I, yielding less ATP. Total: ~10 H⁺ pumped per NADH, ~6 H⁺ per FADH₂. ATP synthase requires ~4 H⁺ per ATP synthesized (3 for synthesis, 1 for transport).

image.alt: Detailed diagram of mitochondrial electron transport chain with proton pumping stoichiometry
image.caption: Complex I (NADH→Q, 4 H⁺), Complex II (FADH₂→Q, 0 H⁺), Complex III (Q→cyt c, 4 H⁺), Complex IV (cyt c→O₂, 2 H⁺). Ubiquinone (Q) and cytochrome c are mobile carriers. Proton gradient drives ATP synthase.

examples.explanation: Mitochondria continuously undergo fission and fusion to maintain health, distribute throughout cells, and isolate damaged components. Fusion (mediated by mitofusins Mfn1/2 on outer membrane and OPA1 on inner membrane) allows content mixing, complementation of mtDNA mutations, and cristae remodeling. Fission (mediated by Drp1 recruited by Fis1/Mff receptors) enables mitochondrial division, distribution to daughter cells during mitosis, and isolation of damaged segments for mitophagy. Imbalance causes disease: excessive fission fragments mitochondria (neurodegeneration); excessive fusion creates hyperfused networks (cancer metabolism).

image.alt: Diagram of mitochondrial fission and fusion machinery
image.caption: Fusion: Mfn1/2 tether outer membranes; OPA1 mediates inner membrane fusion. Fission: Drp1 oligomerizes around mitochondrion, constricts with actin/ER contact sites, divides organelle. Balance maintains mitochondrial health.

classifications.value: Capture light energy to fix atmospheric CO₂ into carbohydrates via photosynthesis; found in plants and algae; generate ATP, NADPH, and organic carbon for cellular metabolism
characteristics: Double-membrane envelope: outer membrane (permeable porins), inner membrane (selective transporters)
Thylakoid membrane system: flattened sacs stacked into grana (appressed regions) connected by stroma lamellae (non-appressed)
Three compartments: intermembrane space, stroma (aqueous interior), thylakoid lumen
Stroma contains Calvin cycle enzymes, chloroplast DNA nucleoids, 70S ribosomes, starch granules
Thylakoid membranes contain photosystems I/II, cytochrome b₆f, ATP synthase, and light-harvesting complexes
classifications.behavior: Chloroplasts convert light energy through two phases: light-dependent reactions (thylakoid membranes) generate ATP and NADPH while splitting water to release O₂; light-independent reactions (Calvin cycle in stroma) use ATP/NADPH to fix CO₂ into carbohydrates. Proton gradient across thylakoid membrane (lumen pH ~5, stroma pH ~8) drives ATP synthesis. Chloroplasts also synthesize amino acids, lipids, and phytohormones; store transient starch; and communicate with nucleus via retrograde signaling. Chloroplasts reposition within cells to optimize light capture and avoid photodamage.

examples.process: Light-Dependent Reactions: Linear and Cyclic Electron Flow
examples.explanation: Photosystem II (PSII) absorbs photons, exciting P680 chlorophyll. Electrons pass through pheophytin, plastoquinone (PQ), cytochrome b₆f (pumps H⁺ into lumen), plastocyanin (PC), to Photosystem I (PSI). PSI re-excites electrons, which reduce ferredoxin, then NADP⁺ to NADPH via ferredoxin-NADP⁺ reductase (FNR). Water splitting at PSII oxygen-evolving complex (OEC) replenishes electrons, releasing O₂ and H⁺. Cyclic flow around PSI generates additional ATP without NADPH production, balancing ATP/NADPH ratio for Calvin cycle demands.

image.alt: Z-scheme diagram of photosynthetic electron transport with energy levels
image.caption: PSII (P680) → PQ → cyt b₆f → PC → PSI (P700) → Fd → FNR → NADPH. Water splitting at OEC provides electrons. Proton gradient from water splitting + cyt b₆f pumping drives ATP synthase.

examples.explanation: Chloroplasts reposition within cells to optimize light capture. Under low light, they spread along periclinal walls (accumulation response) to maximize absorption. Under high light, they move to anticlinal walls (avoidance response) to minimize photodamage. Phototropins (blue-light receptors) mediate these movements via actin filament reorganization. Additionally, non-photochemical quenching (NPQ) dissipates excess excitation energy as heat via the xanthophyll cycle (violaxanthin → antheraxanthin → zeaxanthin), protecting photosystems from oxidative damage.

image.alt: Chloroplast positioning under different light conditions
image.caption: Low light: chloroplasts spread along cell surface (accumulation). High light: chloroplasts move to cell edges (avoidance). Xanthophyll cycle (right) dissipates excess energy as heat to prevent photodamage.

classifications.type: Peroxisomes: Metabolic Partners in Energy Processing
classifications.value: Single-membrane organelles that cooperate with mitochondria and chloroplasts in fatty acid oxidation, photorespiration, and reactive oxygen species metabolism; not of endosymbiotic origin but functionally integrated
characteristics: Single membrane without genome or ribosomes; all proteins imported from cytosol
Contains oxidases producing H₂O₂ and catalase that decomposes H₂O₂ to H₂O + O₂
Performs β-oxidation of very-long-chain fatty acids (mitochondria handle shorter chains)
In plants, participates in photorespiration with chloroplasts and mitochondria
Proliferates by division from pre-existing peroxisomes; proteins imported via PEX receptors
classifications.behavior: Peroxisomes complement mitochondrial and chloroplast metabolism. In animal cells, peroxisomal β-oxidation shortens very-long-chain fatty acids before mitochondrial oxidation. In plant leaf cells, peroxisomes participate in photorespiration: glycolate from chloroplasts is converted to glycine, transported to mitochondria, then to serine with CO₂ release—recovering carbon from Rubisco's oxygenation reaction. Peroxisomes also detoxify reactive oxygen species generated by mitochondria and chloroplasts, protecting cells from oxidative damage.

examples.explanation: When Rubisco fixes O₂ instead of CO₂ (photorespiration), 2-phosphoglycolate is produced, which is toxic if accumulated. In chloroplasts, phosphatase removes phosphate to form glycolate. Glycolate exits to peroxisome, where glycolate oxidase converts it to glyoxylate (producing H₂O₂, decomposed by catalase). Glyoxylate is transaminated to glycine. Two glycines enter mitochondrion, where glycine decarboxylase complex releases CO₂ and NH₃, producing serine. Serine returns to peroxisome, then chloroplast, recovering 75% of carbon. This process consumes energy but prevents carbon loss and toxicity.

image.alt: Photorespiratory pathway showing chloroplast-peroxisome-mitochondrion cooperation
image.caption: Chloroplast: Rubisco oxygenation → 2-phosphoglycolate → glycolate. Peroxisome: glycolate → glyoxylate → glycine. Mitochondrion: 2 glycine → serine + CO₂ + NH₃. Serine returns to chloroplast via peroxisome.

sections.section_title: Fundamental Principles of Organelle Energy Conversion

principles.principle: Chemiosmotic Coupling: Universal Mechanism for Biological Energy Conversion
principles.statement: Mitochondria and chloroplasts both generate ATP through chemiosmosis—the coupling of electron transport to proton gradient formation across membranes, with ATP synthase using proton flow to drive mechanical rotation and chemical synthesis.
principles.application: In mitochondria, electron transport from NADH/FADH₂ to O₂ pumps protons from matrix to intermembrane space, creating Δp ~180-200 mV (ΔΨ ~150-180 mV, ΔpH ~0.5-1 unit). In chloroplasts, light-driven electron transport pumps protons from stroma to thylakoid lumen, creating Δp ~200 mV (ΔΨ ~50 mV, ΔpH ~3-3.5 units). ATP synthase in both organelles has similar structure (F₀ membrane rotor + F₁ catalytic head) and mechanism (binding change mechanism with 3 catalytic sites cycling through loose, tight, open conformations).
principles.example: The antibiotic oligomycin inhibits mitochondrial ATP synthase by binding F₀ subunit, blocking proton flow and halting ATP synthesis. Similarly, DCCD inhibits chloroplast ATP synthase. This conservation demonstrates the ancient origin of chemiosmotic mechanism, predating the divergence of mitochondria and chloroplasts.
principles.analogy: Chemiosmosis is like a hydroelectric dam: electron transport is the pump that moves water (protons) uphill to create a reservoir (proton gradient). ATP synthase is the turbine that generates electricity (ATP) as water flows back downhill. The dam structure (membrane) prevents leakage, maintaining the gradient for on-demand energy production.

image.alt: Side-by-side comparison of chemiosmotic mechanisms in mitochondria and chloroplasts
image.caption: Mitochondria (left): NADH/FADH₂ oxidation pumps H⁺ to intermembrane space; ATP synthase uses gradient for ATP. Chloroplasts (right): Light-driven electron transport pumps H⁺ to thylakoid lumen; ATP synthase uses gradient for ATP. Both use rotational catalysis mechanism.

principles.principle: Endosymbiotic Gene Transfer Creates Nuclear-Organelle Interdependence
principles.statement: Over evolutionary time, most ancestral organelle genes were transferred to the nuclear genome, creating obligate interdependence: organelles require nuclear-encoded proteins for function, while the nucleus depends on organelle energy and metabolites.
principles.application: Human mitochondrial genome encodes only 13 proteins (all ETC subunits), 22 tRNAs, and 2 rRNAs—less than 1% of mitochondrial proteome (~1500 proteins). Chloroplast genomes encode ~100-200 proteins, also a small fraction of total chloroplast proteome (~3000 proteins). Nuclear-encoded organelle proteins are synthesized on cytosolic ribosomes with N-terminal targeting sequences (mitochondrial presequences, chloroplast transit peptides) that direct import via TOM/TIM (mitochondria) or TOC/TIC (chloroplast) translocons.
principles.example: Cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV) has 13 subunits: 3 encoded by mtDNA (catalytic core), 10 encoded by nuclear DNA (structural and assembly factors). Both genomes must be coordinately expressed for functional complex assembly. Mutations in either genome cause mitochondrial disease—demonstrating the functional integration of two genetic systems.
principles.analogy: Nuclear-organelle interdependence is like a corporation with headquarters (nucleus) and specialized factories (organelles). Headquarters designs most equipment and sends blueprints (mRNA) to factories, but factories retain some critical proprietary designs (organelle DNA) and send status reports (retrograde signals) back to headquarters. Both must coordinate for successful operation.

image.alt: Diagram showing gene transfer from organelles to nucleus over evolutionary time
image.caption: Ancestral endosymbiont had ~2000-3000 genes. Modern mitochondria: ~13-40 genes (animals-plants). Modern chloroplasts: ~100-200 genes. Most genes transferred to nucleus; proteins imported back to organelles via targeting sequences.

principles.principle: Retrograde Signaling Coordinates Organelle and Nuclear Gene Expression
principles.statement: Organelles communicate their functional status to the nucleus through retrograde signaling pathways, enabling coordinated gene expression in response to metabolic demands, stress, and developmental cues.
principles.application: Mitochondrial retrograde signaling involves metabolites (citrate, acetyl-CoA, ROS), calcium flux, and proteins (ATFS-1 in C. elegans, CHOP in mammals) that translocate to nucleus and modulate transcription. Chloroplast retrograde signaling involves tetrapyrrole intermediates (Mg-protoporphyrin IX), redox state (plastoquinone pool), and reactive oxygen species that regulate nuclear photosynthesis genes. These pathways ensure nuclear-encoded organelle proteins are produced only when organelles are functional and can import them.
principles.example: Under mitochondrial stress (e.g., ETC inhibition), unfolded proteins accumulate in matrix, activating UPRᵐᵗ (mitochondrial unfolded protein response). ATFS-1 transcription factor, normally imported into mitochondria and degraded, accumulates in cytosol and enters nucleus to upregulate mitochondrial chaperones and proteases. This restores proteostasis or triggers apoptosis if damage is irreparable.
principles.analogy: Retrograde signaling is like a factory sending production reports to headquarters. If machines break down (organelle stress), the factory sends alerts (signals) requesting maintenance crews (chaperones) or new equipment (proteins). Headquarters adjusts production schedules (gene expression) based on factory capacity, preventing wasted resources.

image.alt: Mitochondrial and chloroplast retrograde signaling pathways to nucleus
image.caption: Mitochondria (left): ROS, metabolites, Ca²⁺, unfolded proteins → transcription factors (ATFS-1, CHOP) → nucleus → stress response genes. Chloroplasts (right): Mg-ProtoIX, redox state, ROS → transcription factors (GUN1, PTM) → nucleus → photosynthesis genes.

sections.section_title: Key Components of Energy Conversion Machinery

components.definition: Mitochondrial and chloroplast energy conversion requires coordinated action of electron transport chain complexes, mobile electron carriers, ATP synthase, metabolite transporters, and protein import machinery.
components.equation: Proton motive force: $\Delta p = \Delta\Psi - 59\Delta\text{pH}$ (mV at 25°C); Free energy: $\Delta G = -nF\Delta p$
components.interpretation: The proton motive force (Δp) has two components: electrical potential (ΔΨ, membrane voltage) and chemical potential (ΔpH, proton concentration gradient). In mitochondria, ΔΨ dominates (~150-180 mV); in chloroplasts, ΔpH dominates (~180 mV from 3 pH units). The free energy available from proton flow drives ATP synthesis: ~20 kJ/mol per proton, requiring 3-4 protons per ATP (ΔG for ATP synthesis ~50-60 kJ/mol under cellular conditions).

image.alt: Diagram showing ΔΨ and ΔpH contributions to proton motive force in mitochondria vs. chloroplasts
image.caption: Mitochondria: ΔΨ ~150-180 mV (75%), ΔpH ~30-60 mV (25%). Chloroplasts: ΔΨ ~50 mV (25%), ΔpH ~150 mV (75%). Total Δp ~200 mV in both. Different strategies reflect different membrane properties and functional requirements.

categories.description: ATP synthase (Complex V) is a molecular motor that converts proton gradient energy into chemical bond energy through rotational catalysis, with conserved structure and mechanism across mitochondria, chloroplasts, and bacteria.
categories.detailed_mechanism: ATP synthase has two domains: F₀ (membrane-embedded proton channel with c-ring rotor) and F₁ (catalytic head with α₃β₃ hexamer and γ-subunit stalk). Proton flow through F₀ causes c-ring rotation, transmitted via γ-subunit to F₁. The γ-subunit rotates within α₃β₃, inducing conformational changes in three β-subunits: Open (O, releases ATP), Loose (L, binds ADP + Pi), Tight (T, catalyzes ATP formation). Each 360° rotation produces 3 ATP. Mitochondrial c-rings have 8 subunits (8 H⁺/3 ATP = 2.7 H⁺/ATP); chloroplast c-rings have 14 subunits (14 H⁺/3 ATP = 4.7 H⁺/ATP), reflecting different bioenergetic requirements.

examples.process: Binding Change Mechanism of ATP Synthesis
examples.explanation: Paul Boyer's binding change mechanism (Nobel Prize 1997) explains how rotation drives ATP synthesis without direct chemical coupling. As γ-subunit rotates 120°, each β-subunit cycles through O→L→T→O conformations. ADP + Pi bind to L-site; rotation converts L→T, forcing substrates together to form ATP; rotation converts T→O, releasing ATP. The energy from proton flow is used for product release, not bond formation—the equilibrium favors ATP formation when ADP + Pi are concentrated in the catalytic site.

image.alt: ATP synthase rotational catalysis showing conformational changes in β-subunits
image.caption: γ-subunit rotation (arrows) drives β-subunit conformational changes: O (open, ATP release) → L (loose, ADP+Pi binding) → T (tight, ATP synthesis). Each 120° step produces 1 ATP; full rotation produces 3 ATP.

examples.explanation: ATP synthase can be inhibited by oligomycin (binds F₀, blocks proton channel) or DCCD (modifies c-subunit carboxyl group). Uncouplers (2,4-dinitrophenol, FCCP) are protonophores that shuttle protons across membrane, dissipating gradient without ATP synthesis—energy released as heat. Brown adipose tissue uses uncoupling protein 1 (UCP1) for thermogenesis: protons leak back to matrix without ATP synthesis, generating heat for non-shivering thermogenesis in infants and hibernating mammals.

image.alt: Mechanisms of ATP synthase inhibition and uncoupling
image.caption: Normal (left): proton flow drives ATP synthesis. Oligomycin (center): blocks F₀ channel, halts synthesis. Uncoupler (right): protons bypass ATP synthase, gradient dissipates as heat—no ATP produced.

categories.type: Electron Transport Chain Complexes: Redox Energy Conversion
categories.description: Multi-subunit enzyme complexes that transfer electrons through sequential redox reactions, coupling electron flow to proton pumping across membranes.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Mitochondrial Complex I (NADH:ubiquinone oxidoreductase, 45 subunits, 1 MDa) transfers electrons from NADH to ubiquinone via FMN and 8 Fe-S clusters, pumping 4 H⁺. Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase, 4 subunits) transfers electrons from succinate to ubiquinone via FAD and Fe-S clusters, no proton pumping. Complex III (cytochrome bc₁, 11 subunits) uses Q-cycle to transfer electrons from ubiquinol to cytochrome c, pumping 4 H⁺ per 2 electrons. Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase, 13 subunits) transfers electrons from cytochrome c to O₂, reducing it to H₂O, pumping 2 H⁺. Chloroplast equivalents: PSII (water→PQ), cyt b₆f (PQ→PC), PSI (PC→Fd).

examples.explanation: The Q-cycle doubles proton pumping efficiency by processing ubiquinol in two steps. QH₂ binds Qₒ site, transfers one electron to cytochrome c (via Fe-S and cyt c₁), one to ubiquinone at Qᵢ site (via cyt b). First QH₂ oxidation releases 2 H⁺ to intermembrane space, produces Q•⁻ semiquinone at Qᵢ. Second QH₂ repeats, fully reducing Q•⁻ to QH₂ (taking 2 H⁺ from matrix). Net: 2 QH₂ oxidized, 1 QH₂ regenerated, 4 H⁺ pumped, 2 cyt c reduced. This bifurcated electron flow maximizes proton gradient per electron pair.

image.alt: Q-cycle mechanism in Complex III showing electron bifurcation
image.caption: QH₂ at Qₒ site: one electron to cyt c (high potential), one to Q at Qᵢ site (low potential via cyt b). Two cycles produce 4 H⁺ pumped, 2 cyt c reduced, regenerate 1 QH₂.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Calculating ATP Yield from Glucose Oxidation

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive ATP Accounting for Complete Glucose Oxidation in Mitochondria
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the theoretical maximum ATP yield from complete oxidation of one glucose molecule in a mammalian cell, including glycolysis, pyruvate oxidation, citric acid cycle, and oxidative phosphorylation. Account for: (a) substrate-level phosphorylation, (b) NADH and FADH₂ production, (c) proton stoichiometry for ATP synthesis, (d) transport costs for cytosolic NADH and metabolites. Compare to chloroplast ATP requirements for fixing the equivalent carbon via Calvin cycle.

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate ATP from substrate-level phosphorylation (direct ATP/GTP synthesis without ETC).
step_by_step.calculation: Glycolysis: 2 ATP (net, 4 produced - 2 invested)\nCitric Acid Cycle: 2 GTP (equivalent to ATP)\nTotal substrate-level: 4 ATP

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate reducing equivalents (NADH, FADH₂) produced per glucose.
step_by_step.calculation:
Glycolysis: 2 NADH (cytosolic)
Pyruvate oxidation: 2 NADH (mitochondrial)
Citric Acid Cycle: 6 NADH + 2 FADH₂ (mitochondrial)
Total: 10 NADH + 2 FADH₂

step_by_step.explanation: Account for cytosolic NADH transport into mitochondria. Two shuttles exist: malate-aspartate (yields mitochondrial NADH) or glycerol-3-phosphate (yields FADH₂).
step_by_step.calculation: Malate-aspartate shuttle: 2 cytosolic NADH → 2 mitochondrial NADH\nGlycerol-3-phosphate shuttle: 2 cytosolic NADH → 2 FADH₂\nUsing malate-aspartate (more efficient): 10 NADH + 2 FADH₂ total

step_by_step.explanation: Calculate ATP from oxidative phosphorylation using modern P/O ratios (protons per ATP, protons per NADH/FADH₂).
step_by_step.calculation:
Per NADH: 10 H⁺ pumped / 4 H⁺ per ATP = 2.5 ATP
Per FADH₂: 6 H⁺ pumped / 4 H⁺ per ATP = 1.5 ATP
10 NADH × 2.5 = 25 ATP
2 FADH₂ × 1.5 = 3 ATP
Total oxidative phosphorylation: 28 ATP

step_by_step.explanation: Sum total ATP yield and compare to chloroplast requirements for fixing 6 CO₂ (equivalent to one glucose).
step_by_step.calculation:
Mitochondrial total: 4 (substrate) + 28 (oxidative) = 32 ATP
Chloroplast Calvin cycle: 18 ATP + 12 NADPH per 6 CO₂
NADPH equivalent to ~2.5 ATP each: 12 × 2.5 = 30 ATP
Chloroplast total: 18 + 30 = 48 ATP to fix 6 CO₂
Net energy gain from glucose oxidation: 32 ATP produced vs. 48 ATP invested in fixation

worked_examples.final_answer: Complete glucose oxidation yields 32 ATP (4 substrate-level + 28 oxidative phosphorylation) using modern P/O ratios. Older textbooks cite 36-38 ATP based on outdated P/O ratios (3 ATP/NADH, 2 ATP/FADH₂). Chloroplasts require ~48 ATP equivalents to fix 6 CO₂ into one glucose via Calvin cycle. This asymmetry reflects thermodynamic reality: oxidation releases energy (exergonic, -2870 kJ/mol glucose); fixation requires energy input (endergonic, +2870 kJ/mol). The ~1.5:1 ratio (48:32) represents energy lost as heat during metabolic conversions, consistent with second law of thermodynamics.
worked_examples.concept_applied: ATP accounting demonstrates energy conservation in biological systems. Mitochondria capture ~34% of glucose chemical energy as ATP (32 ATP × 50 kJ/mol = 1600 kJ; glucose ΔG = -2870 kJ); remainder released as heat. This efficiency exceeds most human engines (~25%). Understanding stoichiometry is essential for metabolic engineering, disease diagnosis (mitochondrial disorders show reduced ATP yield), and comparing energy strategies across organisms.

sections.role: Coordinated Energy Conversion, Metabolic Integration, and Cellular Homeostasis

components.composition: Protein-rich (75% protein, 25% lipid) with cardiolipin (unique dimeric phospholipid) stabilizing ETC complexes; impermeable to ions and metabolites without transporters; folded into cristae to increase surface area 5-10 fold
components.function: Houses electron transport chain complexes, ATP synthase, and metabolite transporters; generates proton motive force; maintains membrane potential for protein import and calcium uptake
components.key_feature: Cristae junctions regulated by OPA1 and MICOS complex control diffusion between cristae and boundary membrane, optimizing ETC supercomplex organization and preventing proton leak

image.alt: Cryo-ET reconstruction of mitochondrial inner membrane showing cristae organization
image.caption: Cristae membranes (folded) contain densely packed ETC supercomplexes (I-IV) and ATP synthase dimers (rows along cristae edges). Cardiolipin (red) stabilizes complexes. Cristae junctions (narrow openings) regulated by MICOS complex.

components.composition: Galactolipid-rich (80% MGDG/DGDG, non-bilayer lipids promoting curvature); contains photosystems, cytochrome b₆f, ATP synthase; organized into grana stacks (appressed) and stroma lamellae (non-appressed)
components.function: Site of light-dependent reactions; generates proton gradient across membrane (lumen acidic); separates PSII (grana) from PSI (stroma lamellae) for efficient electron flow
components.key_feature: Lateral heterogeneity: PSII and LHCII enriched in grana appressed regions; PSI and ATP synthase in stroma-exposed regions; prevents short-circuiting of electron flow

image.alt: Thylakoid membrane showing protein distribution between grana and stroma lamellae
image.caption: Grana stacks (appressed): PSII (red), LHCII (orange). Stroma lamellae (non-appressed): PSI (blue), ATP synthase (green), cyt b₆f (purple). Lateral separation optimizes electron flow and prevents energy waste.

components.composition: Circular DNA (mtDNA: 16.6 kb human, 200-2000 kb plants; cpDNA: 120-160 kb) without histones; 70S ribosomes (mitochondrial: 55S in mammals); polycistronic transcription units
components.function: Encodes essential ETC/photosynthesis subunits, rRNAs, tRNAs; enables rapid local response to organelle stress; retained genes mostly hydrophobic membrane proteins difficult to import
components.key_feature: Polyploid: multiple genome copies per organelle (1000s per cell); heteroplasmy (mixed wild-type and mutant genomes) affects disease severity; threshold effect determines phenotypic expression

image.alt: Mitochondrial and chloroplast genome maps with gene annotations
image.caption: Human mtDNA (left): 13 proteins, 22 tRNAs, 2 rRNAs. Chloroplast DNA (right): ~130 genes including photosystem subunits, Rubisco large subunit, rRNAs, tRNAs. Both lack introns (mostly) and use bacterial-type genetic code.

function.operation: Mitochondria oxidize organic fuels through citric acid cycle and ETC, generating proton gradient for ATP synthesis; chloroplasts capture light energy to generate ATP/NADPH and fix CO₂ into carbohydrates; both organelles exchange metabolites with cytosol and each other to coordinate cellular metabolism.
function.energy: Mitochondria: ~30-32 ATP per glucose (ΔG = -2870 kJ/mol); efficiency ~34%. Chloroplasts: ~48 ATP equivalents to fix 6 CO₂ (ΔG = +2870 kJ/mol); solar conversion efficiency ~3-6% in crops, up to 10% in optimal conditions.
function.mechanism: Chemiosmotic coupling via proton gradients; electron transport through redox carriers; rotational catalysis by ATP synthase; metabolite exchange via specific transporters (mitochondrial carriers, chloroplast envelope translocons).

image.alt: Integrated diagram showing energy and metabolite flow between mitochondria, chloroplasts, and cytosol
image.caption: Chloroplasts (top): light → ATP/NADPH → Calvin cycle → carbohydrates. Mitochondria (bottom): carbohydrates → pyruvate → citric acid cycle → ETC → ATP. Cytosol (center): glycolysis, metabolite exchange. Both organelles communicate via retrograde signals.

cycle.operation: Pyruvate/fatty acids enter matrix → citric acid cycle generates NADH/FADH₂ → ETC oxidizes carriers, pumps protons → ATP synthase produces ATP → ADP/Pi recycled
cycle.purpose: Convert chemical energy from organic molecules into universal energy currency (ATP) for cellular work

image.alt: Citric acid cycle and oxidative phosphorylation integration
image.caption: Acetyl-CoA → citric acid cycle → 3 NADH + 1 FADH₂ + 1 GTP per turn. ETC oxidizes carriers → proton gradient → ATP synthase. Oxygen is terminal electron acceptor.

cycle.operation: Light reactions generate ATP/NADPH → Calvin cycle fixes CO₂ into G3P → G3P exported for sucrose/starch synthesis → ADP/NADP⁺ recycled to light reactions
cycle.purpose: Convert light energy into chemical energy stored in carbohydrates; produce organic carbon for cellular metabolism

image.alt: Calvin cycle showing ATP/NADPH consumption and carbon flow
image.caption: 3 CO₂ + 9 ATP + 6 NADPH → 1 G3P + 9 ADP + 8 Pi + 6 NADP⁺. G3P exported for sucrose (cytosol) or starch (chloroplast). Regeneration of RuBP requires 5/6 G3P.

sections.section_title: Process Flow: Mitochondrial Protein Import and Assembly
sections.introduction: Nuclear-encoded mitochondrial proteins (~99% of mitochondrial proteome) are synthesized on cytosolic ribosomes and imported via sophisticated translocon systems. This process requires targeting sequences, chaperone assistance, membrane potential, and ATP hydrolysis, with distinct pathways for different mitochondrial compartments.

process_flow.description: Nuclear-encoded mitochondrial proteins are synthesized on free cytosolic ribosomes with N-terminal presequences (20-50 amino acids, amphipathic α-helix, positively charged).
process_flow.mechanism: Cytosolic chaperones (Hsp70, Hsp90) bind nascent chains, preventing aggregation and maintaining import-competent unfolded state. 14-3-3 proteins may stabilize presequences. Import receptors on outer membrane (Tom20, Tom22) recognize presequences via hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions.
process_flow.result: Import-competent precursor protein delivered to mitochondrial surface; aggregation prevented; targeting specificity ensured
applications: Prevents cytosolic aggregation of hydrophobic proteins

image.alt: Cytosolic chaperone maintenance of mitochondrial precursor proteins
image.caption: Cytosolic ribosome synthesizes precursor with presequence (red). Hsp70/Hsp90 (blue) bind, maintain unfolded state. 14-3-3 (green) stabilizes. Complex delivered to Tom20/Tom22 receptors on outer membrane.

process_flow.process: Outer Membrane Translocation via TOM Complex
process_flow.description: Precursor proteins cross outer membrane through TOM (Translocase of Outer Membrane) complex, the main entry gate for mitochondrial proteins.
process_flow.mechanism: Tom20/Tom22 receptors bind presequence, transfer to Tom40 channel (β-barrel protein). Precursor threads through Tom40 in unfolded state. Small Tim chaperones in intermembrane space bind hydrophobic segments, preventing backsliding and guiding to inner membrane translocons. ATP hydrolysis by cytosolic Hsp70 provides driving force.
process_flow.result: Protein crosses outer membrane into intermembrane space; ready for sorting to appropriate compartment
applications: Universal entry point for mitochondrial proteins

image.alt: TOM complex structure and translocation mechanism
image.caption: Tom20/Tom22 (receptors) bind presequence → transfer to Tom40 (channel) → protein threads through → small Tims (chaperones) in IMS prevent backsliding → directed to TIM23 or TIM22.

process_flow.process: Inner Membrane Translocation via TIM Complexes
process_flow.description: Proteins sorted to matrix use TIM23 complex; carrier proteins use TIM22 complex; different mechanisms for different destinations.
process_flow.mechanism: TIM23: Presequence binds Tim50, protein translocates through Tim23 channel. Matrix Hsp70 (mtHsp70) binds incoming chain, undergoes ATP-driven conformational changes (Brownian ratchet) to pull protein into matrix. Membrane potential (ΔΨ) across inner membrane drives presequence translocation. TIM22: Multi-pass inner membrane proteins (metabolite carriers) insert laterally from TIM22 complex without entering matrix.
process_flow.result: Matrix proteins fully imported; inner membrane proteins inserted; intermembrane space proteins released
applications: Energy coupling via membrane potential and ATP

image.alt: TIM23 and TIM22 translocation pathways
image.caption: TIM23 (left): presequence proteins → matrix, driven by ΔΨ and mtHsp70. TIM22 (right): carrier proteins → inner membrane, lateral insertion. Both require ATP and membrane potential.

process_flow.description: Matrix-targeting presequences are cleaved; proteins fold with chaperone assistance; some assemble into complexes.
process_flow.mechanism: Mitochondrial processing peptidase (MPP) cleaves presequence upon matrix entry. Additional peptidases (Oct1, Icp55) may further process N-termini. Matrix chaperones (mtHsp70, Hsp60/Hsp10) assist folding. Assembly factors help incorporate proteins into ETC complexes. Misfolded proteins degraded by AAA proteases (m-AAA, i-AAA) in inner membrane.
process_flow.result: Mature protein sequence; properly folded; assembled into functional complexes; quality control ensures only functional proteins persist
applications: Quality control prevents defective complex assembly

image.alt: Presequence cleavage and chaperone-assisted folding in mitochondrial matrix
image.caption: MPP cleaves presequence (step 1). mtHsp70 releases (step 2). Hsp60/Hsp10 assist folding (step 3). Assembly factors incorporate into ETC complexes (step 4). Misfolded proteins degraded by AAA proteases.

sections.section_title: Advanced Concepts: Organelle Dynamics and Quality Control
sections.definition: Mitochondria and chloroplasts are dynamic organelles that continuously undergo fission, fusion, movement, and turnover to maintain function, respond to cellular demands, and eliminate damaged components through quality control pathways.

process_types.type: Mitochondrial Quality Control: Mitophagy and UPR
process_types.energy_profile: ATP-dependent (chaperone function, proteasomal degradation, autophagy); GTP-dependent (fission/fusion dynamics)
process_types.direction: Quality control pathway eliminating damaged mitochondria and restoring proteostasis
process_types.function: Detect and eliminate dysfunctional mitochondria to prevent ROS overproduction, cytochrome c release, and cell death; restore proteostasis through chaperone upregulation
goals: Identify damaged mitochondria via membrane potential loss or protein aggregation
Tag with ubiquitin for autophagic recognition
Upregulate chaperones via UPRᵐᵗ if stress is moderate
process_types.energy_source: ATP for chaperones (Hsp70, Hsp60), proteasome, autophagy machinery; GTP for Drp1 fission

primary_example.explanation: Healthy mitochondria import PINK1 kinase into inner membrane where it's cleaved and degraded. Damaged mitochondria lose membrane potential, preventing PINK1 import. PINK1 accumulates on outer membrane, phosphorylating ubiquitin and recruiting Parkin E3 ubiquitin ligase. Parkin ubiquitinates outer membrane proteins (Mfn1/2, VDAC), creating ubiquitin chains. Autophagy receptors (p62, OPTN) bind ubiquitin and LC3 on autophagosome membrane, engulfing mitochondrion. Autophagosome fuses with lysosome for degradation. Mutations in PINK1 or Parkin cause early-onset Parkinson's disease due to accumulated damaged mitochondria in neurons.

image.alt: PINK1-Parkin pathway for mitochondrial quality control
image.caption: Healthy (left): PINK1 imported, degraded. Damaged (right): PINK1 accumulates, recruits Parkin → ubiquitination → autophagy receptors → autophagosome → lysosomal degradation.

examples.explanation: Accumulation of unfolded proteins in matrix activates UPRᵐᵗ. In C. elegans, ATFS-1 transcription factor normally imported into mitochondria and degraded. Under stress, import efficiency decreases, ATFS-1 accumulates in cytosol and enters nucleus, upregulating mitochondrial chaperones (Hsp60, Hsp10), proteases (Lon, ClpP), and ETC assembly factors. In mammals, CHOP, ATF4, and ATF5 mediate similar responses. If stress persists, UPRᵐᵗ switches to apoptotic signaling via cytochrome c release.

image.alt: Mitochondrial unfolded protein response signaling pathway
image.caption: Unfolded proteins accumulate → ATFS-1 import blocked → ATFS-1 enters nucleus → chaperone/protease gene upregulation → proteostasis restored or apoptosis if irreversible.

image.alt: Integrated mitochondrial quality control pathways
image.caption: Fission/fusion balance isolates damage. PINK1-Parkin tags for mitophagy. UPRᵐᵗ upregulates chaperones. Proteases degrade damaged proteins. Apoptosis eliminates cells with irreparable damage.

process_types.type: Chloroplast Quality Control: Chlorophagy and Retrograde Signaling
process_types.direction: Quality control pathway eliminating damaged chloroplasts and adjusting nuclear gene expression
process_types.function: Remove photodamaged chloroplasts, adjust nuclear photosynthesis gene expression based on chloroplast status, prevent ROS overproduction from damaged photosystems
goals: Detect photodamage via ROS production and protein aggregation
Degrade damaged D1 protein of PSII via FtsH proteases
Remove entire damaged chloroplasts via chlorophagy
Signal nucleus to adjust photosynthesis gene expression
Balance repair vs. replacement based on damage severity
process_types.energy_source: ATP for chaperones (Hsp70, Hsp90), proteases (FtsH, Clp), autophagy; light for signaling activation

primary_example.process: D1 Protein Turnover and PSII Repair Cycle
primary_example.explanation: PSII reaction center D1 protein is highly susceptible to photodamage from reactive oxygen species. Damaged D1 is recognized by FtsH protease complex in thylakoid membrane, degraded, and replaced with newly synthesized D1. This repair cycle occurs continuously under light, with D1 half-life ~1-4 hours depending on light intensity. PSII partially disassembles (removes CP43 subunit) to allow D1 access. New D1 inserted co-translationally on thylakoid-bound ribosomes. Assembly factors (LPA1, LPA2) assist reassembly. If damage exceeds repair capacity, entire chloroplast may be removed via chlorophagy.

image.alt: PSII repair cycle showing D1 protein turnover
image.caption: Photodamage → D1 oxidation → PSII disassembly (CP43 removal) → FtsH protease degrades D1 → new D1 synthesized/inserted → PSII reassembly → functional PSII restored.

image.alt: Chloroplast quality control mechanisms
image.caption: D1 repair cycle for PSII. Chlorophagy for entire organelle removal. Retrograde signaling (GUN1, PTM) adjusts nuclear gene expression. Antioxidant systems (ascorbate, glutathione) scavenge ROS.

sections.relationship: Mitochondrial and chloroplast quality control pathways share common principles (chaperone-mediated folding, protease-mediated degradation, autophagic removal) but are adapted to organelle-specific stresses (oxidative stress in mitochondria, photodamage in chloroplasts). Both communicate with nucleus via retrograde signaling to coordinate gene expression. Failure of quality control leads to organelle dysfunction, ROS overproduction, and disease (neurodegeneration for mitochondria, reduced photosynthesis for chloroplasts).

sections.section_title: Molecular Mechanisms: Regulation of Organelle Biogenesis and Activity
sections.purpose: Mitochondrial and chloroplast biogenesis and activity are regulated by nuclear transcription factors, post-translational modifications, metabolite signaling, and environmental cues to match cellular energy demands with organelle capacity.

activation.definition: Organelle biogenesis is activated by transcriptional programs that upregulate nuclear-encoded organelle proteins, coordinated with organelle genome expression and protein import capacity.
activation.effect: Mitochondrial biogenesis: PGC-1α coactivator responds to exercise, cold, fasting by activating NRF1/2, ERRα transcription factors, upregulating ETC subunits, TCA enzymes, and import machinery. Chloroplast biogenesis: GLK transcription factors respond to light signals, upregulating photosynthesis genes; chloroplast development from proplastids requires coordinated nuclear and plastid gene expression.

image.alt: Transcriptional regulation of mitochondrial and chloroplast biogenesis
image.caption: Mitochondria (left): PGC-1α → NRF1/2, ERRα → nuclear genes for ETC, TCA, import. Chloroplasts (right): Light → phytochrome/cryptochrome → GLK → nuclear photosynthesis genes. Both require coordinated organelle genome expression.

key_properties.explanation: Organelles sense cellular energy status via AMP/ATP ratio, NAD⁺/NADH ratio, and acetyl-CoA levels. AMPK (AMP-activated protein kinase) activated by high AMP/ATP phosphorylates targets to increase catabolism (fatty acid oxidation, glucose uptake) and decrease anabolism. SIRT1 deacetylase activated by high NAD⁺ (fasting) deacetylates PGC-1α, enhancing mitochondrial biogenesis. Acetyl-CoA levels regulate histone acetylation, linking metabolism to epigenetic gene regulation. This creates feedback loops matching organelle capacity to energy demand.

image.alt: Metabolic sensors regulating mitochondrial biogenesis and activity
image.caption: AMP/ATP ratio → AMPK → catabolism up, anabolism down. NAD⁺/NADH ratio → SIRT1 → PGC-1α deacetylation → biogenesis. Acetyl-CoA → histone acetylation → gene expression. Integrated sensing matches capacity to demand.

key_properties.explanation: Mitochondria take up calcium via MCU (mitochondrial calcium uniporter) at ER-mitochondria contact sites (MAMs—mitochondria-associated membranes). Calcium stimulates dehydrogenases (pyruvate, isocitrate, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase) to match TCA cycle flux to cellular activity. Excessive calcium triggers permeability transition pore opening, cytochrome c release, and apoptosis. Chloroplasts also regulate calcium for signaling, with stromal calcium affecting Calvin cycle enzymes and starch metabolism. ER-mitochondria-chloroplast contact sites enable direct metabolite and signal exchange without cytosolic diffusion.

image.alt: Calcium signaling between ER, mitochondria, and chloroplasts
image.caption: ER releases Ca²⁺ via IP₃ receptors → mitochondria take up via MCU at MAMs → stimulates TCA cycle. Excessive Ca²⁺ → permeability transition → apoptosis. Chloroplasts also regulate Ca²⁺ for metabolic control.

key_properties.property: Reactive Oxygen Species: Signaling vs. Damage
key_properties.explanation: Mitochondria and chloroplasts produce ROS (superoxide, H₂O₂) as byproducts of electron transport. At low levels, ROS function as signaling molecules (mitohormesis), activating stress response pathways that enhance defense capacity. At high levels, ROS damage proteins, lipids, and DNA, contributing to aging and disease. Antioxidant systems (superoxide dismutase, catalase, glutathione, ascorbate) maintain ROS balance. Mitochondrial SOD2 (MnSOD) converts superoxide to H₂O₂; catalase and peroxiredoxins decompose H₂O₂. Chloroplasts use ascorbate-glutathione cycle. The dual nature of ROS (signaling vs. damage) requires precise regulation.

image.alt: Reactive oxygen species production and scavenging in organelles
image.caption: Low ROS: signaling (redox-sensitive transcription factors, kinase activation). High ROS: damage (protein oxidation, lipid peroxidation, DNA mutations). Antioxidants (SOD, catalase, glutathione) maintain balance. Mitohormesis: mild stress enhances defense capacity.

summary: Mitochondria and chloroplasts are semi-autonomous organelles of endosymbiotic origin that serve as the primary energy-converting systems in eukaryotic cells. Mitochondria, derived from α-proteobacteria, generate ATP through oxidative phosphorylation by oxidizing organic molecules via the citric acid cycle and electron transport chain, using oxygen as terminal electron acceptor. Chloroplasts, derived from cyanobacteria, capture light energy through photosynthesis, generating ATP and NADPH via light-dependent reactions and fixing CO₂ into carbohydrates via the Calvin cycle. Both organelles share fundamental features reflecting their bacterial ancestry: double-membrane envelopes, circular genomes, 70S ribosomes, and chemiosmotic ATP synthesis mechanisms. However, extensive endosymbiotic gene transfer has created nuclear-organelle interdependence: organelles retain only essential genes (13 in human mitochondria, ~130 in chloroplasts) while requiring import of thousands of nuclear-encoded proteins via sophisticated translocon systems (TOM/TIM for mitochondria, TOC/TIC for chloroplasts). Energy conversion in both organelles relies on proton gradients across membranes driving ATP synthase through rotational catalysis. Mitochondria generate ~30-32 ATP per glucose with ~34% efficiency; chloroplasts require ~48 ATP equivalents to fix 6 CO₂ into glucose. Both organelles are dynamic, undergoing continuous fission, fusion, and quality control (mitophagy, chlorophagy) to maintain function. Retrograde signaling pathways communicate organelle status to the nucleus, coordinating gene expression with metabolic demands. Dysfunction in these organelles underlies numerous diseases including mitochondrial myopathies, neurodegenerative disorders, and metabolic syndromes, highlighting their essential roles in cellular and organismal homeostasis.
